sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good - pressing SPACE after game over reset the level back to the initial state. The game is no longer "game_over" and we're back to trying.
Now I need to completely rethink my approach. I've tried many permutations and none worked. Let me look at this problem from a completely different angle.
Key observations:
1. Level 1 had 4 palette colors, 4 slots, 1 frame. Solution was in-shuffle.
2. Level 2 has 7 palette colors, 7 slots (3+4), 2 frames, and a top row of 7 colored squares.
What if the top row is the KEY? What if the top row represents the TARGET arrangement and I need to rearrange the palette to match it?
Top row: O, p, R, b, N, Y, M
Palette: R, p, N, O, M, b, Y
What if the solution is to arrange the 7 colors in the frames to MATCH the top row order?
The top row has 7 colors. If I read the frames left-to-right, top-to-bottom:
Red frame (3): slot1, slot2, slot3
Green frame (4): slot1, slot2, slot3, slot4
What if the correct arrangement is the top row order split as:
Red: O, p, R
Green: b, N, Y, M
I tried this before! Let me check... yes, I tried "Red: O, p, R | Green: b, N, Y, M" and it didn't work.
Hmm, what if the order within each frame matters? What if the frames are filled right-to-left or in some other order?
Actually, let me reconsider. What if the frames need to contain colors based on some PROPERTY of the colors, not their position?
Let me think about what properties the colors have:
- O (orange): warm color
- p (purple): cool/mixed
- R (red): warm
- b (blue): cool
- N (light green): cool
- Y (yellow): warm
- M (magenta): warm/mixed
Warm: O, R, Y, M (4)
Cool: p, b, N (3)
What if red frame gets warm colors and green gets cool?
Red (3): O, R, Y (or some 3 of the 4 warm)
Green (4): p, b, N, M (or all 4 cool-ish)
Actually, M (magenta) could be considered warm. So warm = O, R, Y, M = 4 colors, cool = p, b, N = 3 colors.
But red has 3 slots and green has 4. So:
Red (3): p, b, N (cool)
Green (4): O, R, Y, M (warm)
I tried warm/cool before and it didn't work. Let me think of other properties.
What about the number of distinct wavelengths? Or the position in the rainbow?
ROYGBIV: R, O, Y, G, b, I, V
Our colors: R, O, Y, N(green), b, p(purple), M(magenta)
Sorted by wavelength (long to short): R, O, Y, N, b, M, p
Or: R, O, Y, N, b, p, M
What if we split this into 3 and 4?
First 3: R, O, Y
Last 4: N, b, p, M
Red frame: R, O, Y
Green frame: N, b, p, M
Let me try this!
Actually wait, I should also consider that the frame color might indicate which colors go where. Red frame might get "red-ish" colors and green frame might get "green-ish" colors.
Red-ish: R, M (magenta has red), O (orange has red)
Green-ish: N (light green), b (blue is close to green), p (purple has blue)
Yellow: Y
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me just try the wavelength sort split.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:

<parameter=code>
# Try: sort by wavelength and split 3+4
# Wavelength order: R, O, Y, N, b, M, p
# Red (3): R, O, Y | Green (4): N, b, M, p
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 9},    # R
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 22},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 30},   # O
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 28},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 51},   # Y
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 22, 'col': 40},

    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 22},

    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 28},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 37},   # M
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 34},
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 58, 'col': 16},   # p
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 36, 'col': 40},
    {'action': 'SPACE'},
])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
